prs.title: test: add unicode pattern and patternProperties tests for draft2020-12
prs.body:
Adds minimal Unicode literal tests for pattern and patternProperties in draft‑2020‑12.
These tests use a simple non‑ASCII character (π) to confirm literal Unicode matching in the Unicode‑aware regex mode required by draft‑2020‑12, avoiding advanced regex features and focusing on basic literal behavior.

prs.title: Add required + null validation test
prs.body:
This PR adds a minimal test case for the `required` keyword validating a `null` instance.

Per the specification, `required` only applies to object instances, so `null` should be considered valid. This test helps catch potential implementation bugs (e.g. treating `null` as an object in JavaScript).

This change follows the discussion and approval in #821.

prs.title: Clarify optional tests and document integer example
prs.body:
This PR clarifies the purpose and usage of optional tests in the README.
It explains when optional tests should be run, states that failing them does not imply non-compliance, and documents the historical 1.0 integer test (issue #22) as a concrete example.
Fixes #25

prs.title: Clarify that the test-suite is not a style guide.
prs.body:
This PR clarifies the purpose of the JSON Schema Test Suite by explicitly stating that it is intended to verify behaviour defined by the specification, not to serve as a style guide or a set of recommended schema patterns.

The goal is to reduce confusion for both schema authors and implementors by making it clear that the presence of a test does not imply best practice, only that the behaviour is specified.

Fixes #324

prs.title: Add specification links for type keyword tests
prs.body:
Added specification field to the type tests in type.json so each test now points to the official JSON Schema type keyword docs: https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/json-schema-validation#type

This helps anyone reading the tests understand which part of the spec each test is based on. No changes to test logic, just added reference links.

prs.title: Add JSON Schema specification links to type tests
prs.body:
Added `specification` field to the type tests in `type.json` so each test now points to the official JSON Schema `type` keyword docs: https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/json-schema-validation#type

This helps anyone reading the tests understand which part of the spec each test is based on. No changes to test logic, just added reference links.

prs.title: Add if/then/else test cases for issue #767
prs.body:
This PR adds additional tests for correct handling of the `false` schema within
conditional keywords (`if`, `then`, `else`), as described in Issue #767.

### What these tests cover
- When `if` matches, `then: false` must cause validation to fail.
- When `if` does not match, `else: false` must cause validation to fail.
- When both `then` and `else` are `false`, all data must be invalid regardless
  of the branch taken.
- Ensures that `false` is treated as a schema that always fails, matching JSON
  Schema semantics, rather than being interpreted as `{}`.

These tests fill gaps in draft2020-12 conditional logic coverage, addressing the
behavior described in Issue #767.

Fixes #767.

prs.title: Added shapes module with animated circle - Hacktoberfest 2025

Created an animated circle module inside modules/shapes,/shapes,001_byShristi.html

issues.title: Bug: Make SideEditorLink accessible (non-interactive span with click handler) ​
issues.body:
The SideEditorLink component uses a non-interactive span with an onClick handler to focus the editor, but it is not keyboard- or screen‑reader accessible.

Files/areas
app/components/SideEditorLink/SideEditorLink.tsx (around lines 14–24)
​
Problem
SideEditorLink renders a span element with an onClick handler to move focus to the editor.
​
This element does not have role="button", a tabIndex, or keyboard event handlers (Enter/Space), so it cannot be activated via keyboard or announced correctly by assistive technologies.
​

Proposed solution
Change the span to a semantic <button> element for the clickable control.
Update styles as needed so the button matches the existing visual design, instead of using a styled span.
If a non-button element must be retained, add role="button", tabIndex={0}, and an onKeyDown handler that triggers the same behaviour on Enter and Space.
​

I’m happy to open a PR implementing this change if this approach looks acceptable.

issues.title: Remove leftover console.log statements from UI components
issues.body:
There are a few console.log statements left in production components under app/components. These were likely added for debugging but now add unnecessary noise to the browser console.

Details
	•	SideEditorLink logs the editor range when the link is clicked.
	•	TestCaseWindow logs scroll positions during scrolling, which can get very noisy.

Why this matters

Extra console output makes it harder to notice real errors and doesn’t look great in production. It can also expose internal state that users don’t need to see.

Proposed fix
	•	Remove the console.log calls from:
	•	SideEditorLink.tsx
	•	TestCaseWindow.tsx
	•	Do a quick check for similar logs under app/components.

I’m happy to open a PR for this if it sounds good.

issues.title: Tooling: Optional CI suggestion for adding comments to complex tests
issues.body:
Summary

The test schema already supports a comment field at both the test case and individual test level, and CONTRIBUTING encourages using it to explain tests that are unclear or complex. In practice, comments are only present in some files, and there’s no tooling to help surface places where an explanation might be helpful for future readers.

Motivation

- As the test suite grows, some tests encode subtle or non-obvious behaviour that isn’t immediately clear from the schema alone. While comments are already part of the schema and recommended in CONTRIBUTING, it’s easy for contributors to miss adding them, especially when the test “works” but isn’t self-explanatory.
- A small, non-blocking suggestion mechanism could help improve long-term maintainability by nudging contributors to document complex tests, without enforcing style rules or changing validation semantics.

Proposal

Add an optional, warning-only check to bin/jsonschema_suite that scans test files and suggests adding comment fields when a test looks potentially complex or non-obvious.

For example, the check could emit a warning when a test case or test:
	•	Uses schemas with many keywords or deeply nested subschemas
	•	Exercises subtle conditional or edge-case behaviour
	•	Has a long or nuanced description but no accompanying comment

The check should:
	•	Only print warnings (never fail CI)
	•	Clearly state that this is a heuristic suggestion, not a requirement
	•	Emphasize that comments are encouraged for clarity, not mandated by the suite

Implementation sketch

One possible approach would be to extend bin/jsonschema_suite with a subcommand or flag (e.g. lint-comments) that:
	•	Walks test files under tests/**
	•	Applies simple heuristics to flag potentially complex tests lacking a comment
	•	Prints human-readable warnings suggesting adding a comment, possibly with a short reference to the guidance in CONTRIBUTING

This could initially be run locally or as a non-blocking CI job, allowing the heuristics to be tuned based on feedback before deciding whether it belongs in CI long-term.

issues.title: Add Unicode-focused tests for string length and pattern handling
issues.body:
Summary

Right now, string-related keywords like minLength, maxLength, pattern, and propertyNames don’t have much coverage for Unicode strings that clearly exercise the spec’s “length in Unicode code points” semantics. Most Unicode-related cases live in optional/non-bmp-regex.json and optional/ecmascript-regex.json, and those focus more on regex engine features than on core length and basic Unicode handling.

Motivation

- The validation spec and accompanying docs define minLength/maxLength in terms of Unicode code points, not bytes, and they use Unicode examples to illustrate this.
- In practice, implementations often differ when non‑ASCII text is involved, so having a few explicit tests helps confirm that validators are using code‑point length as required.
- The existing optional regex tests show that engine‑specific behaviour is already isolated under optional/, which leaves room for a small number of portable Unicode examples in the core suite.

Proposal

1. Required tests (core)

- Extend tests/*/minLength.json and maxLength.json (for drafts like draft2020‑12 and later) with cases where:

        - Non‑ASCII strings (for example, simple emoji or common non‑Latin text) sit right at or around the length boundary,                making the code‑point counting explicit.
        - The same numeric minLength/maxLength is applied to both ASCII and non‑ASCII examples, so it’s clear that both are measured in code points rather than bytes.

- Extend pattern.json and propertyNames.json with a few straightforward Unicode examples where:

        -  Patterns match literal Unicode characters without relying on flags or advanced Unicode properties.
        -  propertyNames includes keys that contain Unicode letters, again without depending on engine‑specific behaviour.


2.Optional tests

- Under tests/*/optional/ (next to non-bmp-regex.json and ecmascript-regex.json), add a small set of tests for more advanced Unicode scenarios—things like zero‑width characters, combining marks, or RTL sequences—where behaviour is more tied to the regex engine.
- These would be clearly marked as optional and aimed at implementations that want to exercise richer Unicode/regex support beyond what the core spec strictly requires.

Required tests should stay within behaviour clearly mandated by the spec (no grapheme‑cluster rules or engine‑specific flags), with more advanced Unicode and regex behaviour covered under optional/ instead.

issues.title: Expand $defs keyword test coverage
issues.body:
Summary
The $defs keyword tests currently have very limited coverage in tests/draft2019-09/defs.json, tests/draft2020-12/defs.json, and tests/v1/defs.json. These files mainly exercise validating definition schemas against the metaschema, and do not cover many important behaviours and edge cases of $defs specified in the newer drafts.

Motivation

- $defs is the canonical location for reusable subschemas starting in draft 2019-09, replacing definitions.
- Implementations rely on this repository as a conformance test suite, so missing coverage around $defs can hide bugs in reference resolution, name handling, and nested definitions.
- The CONTRIBUTING guidance explicitly welcomes additional tests where the behaviour is clearly specified and broadly applicable.

Problem

At the moment, $defs tests do not cover several behaviours that are both spec-defined and important in practice:
- Invalid or unusual definition names (empty strings, special characters, etc.).
- Nested $defs (e.g., $defs within subschemas, or multiple levels of $defs).
- Circular and cross-references between definitions via $ref.
- Definitions that combine $defs with other validation keywords (type, enum, allOf, etc.).
- Definitions present in $defs that are never referenced from the main schema.
- All of the above are patterns encouraged or allowed by the spec, but they currently lack direct coverage in the suite’s $defs-focused files.

Proposal
Add new test cases to the $defs-focused files for the applicable drafts:

For draft2019-09, draft2020-12, and v1, extend tests/**/defs.json with:

- Test groups for invalid or unusual definition names.
- Test groups for nested $defs structures.
- Test groups with circular and cross-references between definitions.
- Test groups that combine $defs with other validation keywords and boolean schemas.
- Test groups including unreferenced definitions.

All new tests would:

- Keep to the “minimal form” style used elsewhere (no extra unrelated keywords).
- Only cover behaviours that are clearly defined in the corresponding specification text.
- Be added only to drafts where $defs is part of the vocabulary (draft2019-09, draft2020-12, v1), leaving earlier definitions-based drafts unchanged.

issues.title: Missing minContains coverage when contains is false (draft 2020‑12)
issues.body:
Description
In draft 2020‑12, minContains constrains the number of items that match the contains subschema. tests/draft2020-12/minContains.json currently has cases where contains is a normal schema (for example { \"const\": 1 }), but I don’t see a case where contains is the boolean schema false.

When contains is false, the number of matches is always 0, so:

minContains: 0 should pass for any array.

minContains: 1 should always fail, regardless of array length.
​

Proposed change

In tests/draft2020-12/minContains.json, add a test case such as “minContains with contains: false”.
​

Schema: { "contains": false, "minContains": 0 }, instance: [1, 2], valid: true.

Schema: { "contains": false, "minContains": 1 }, instance: [1, 2], valid: false.

If this seems useful, I’d be happy to open a PR adding these tests.

issues.title: Tests/Coverage: Missing coverage for required unknown vocabularies in $vocabulary (draft 2020-12)
issues.body:
Description

In draft 2020-12, $vocabulary distinguishes between optional and required vocabularies. The test suite currently covers the case where an unknown vocabulary is marked as optional (false) and is therefore ignored.

However, I don’t see coverage for the complementary case where an unknown vocabulary is marked as required (true).

The specification states that if $vocabulary specifies a vocabulary URI with value true and the implementation does not recognise it, the implementation MUST refuse to process the schema. This is a stronger requirement than simply ignoring the vocabulary, and I wanted to check whether this behaviour is intentionally left untested, or whether adding coverage here would be considered useful.
Question / Proposed direction

Would it make sense for the test suite to include a case that exercises this scenario — i.e. a schema that declares an unknown required vocabulary and expects schema processing to be refused?

I realize that “refuse to process the schema” is not always straightforward to model in the test suite, since it is a schema-level failure rather than instance validation per se. Because of that, I’m not assuming a particular encoding (for example, whether this would be represented as valid: false or handled in some other way consistent with existing patterns).

One possible approach could be:
	•	Add a test under tests/draft2020-12/ that declares an unknown required vocabulary in $vocabulary.
	•	Assert failure in whatever way the suite currently represents schema refusal, if that’s feasible.

If this isn’t something the suite wants to encode, that’s totally fine — I mainly wanted to confirm whether this omission is intentional.

Happy to work on a PR if there’s agreement on how (or whether) this should be represented.

issues.title: Tooling/CI: Enforce unique test descriptions per draft directory
issues.body:
Description

bin/jsonschema_suite currently enforces uniqueness of test case and test descriptions only within a single file. This means the same description string can appear in multiple files under the same draft directory (for example, tests/draft2020-12/fileA.json and tests/draft2020-12/fileB.json).

It would be helpful if descriptions were also unique across all files in a given draft directory, so that runners and tools can reliably identify a single test (or test case) by its description.

Proposed change

In bin/jsonschema_suite, in the SanityTests checks that currently enforce per-file uniqueness, add a new check (for example, test_all_descriptions_are_unique_across_draft).

For each draft directory under tests/ (e.g. tests/draft7/, tests/draft2019-09/, tests/draft2020-12/, tests/v1/), collect:

All test case "description" values from every .json file.

Optionally, all inner "tests"[]."description" values as well, if we also want uniqueness at that level.

Then:

Assert that each description within that draft directory is unique; fail the sanity check if any duplicates are found.

Wire this new check into the existing check command so it runs in CI along with the other sanity tests.

If this approach looks reasonable, I’m happy to work on a PR adding the new check and fixing any existing duplicates that show up.

issues.title: GSOC 2026 : Make “Optional Tests” Machine-Selectable (Capabilities Manifest + Runner Interop)

labels.name: gsoc
labels.description: Google Summer of Code Project Idea

issues.body:
**Project Title**
Test Suite “Capabilities Manifest” for Optional/Environment-Dependent Tests

**Brief Description**

Add a machine-readable metadata layer to the JSON Schema Test Suite so that runners (including Bowtie) can automatically decide which tests apply to a given implementation or environment. The manifest would describe capabilities such as whether an implementation enables format validation, supports big integers, or uses a network-less remote resolution strategy, and runners could filter tests based on those capabilities. The work includes defining the manifest format, integrating checks into CI, and documenting how implementers can declare and consume these capabilities.

**Why It’s Needed**

The Test Suite README notes that optional/ currently conflates multiple reasons why a test is optional and that the directory structure should be made richer in the future. This makes it hard for runners and tools like Bowtie to automate test selection consistently, because they cannot tell whether a test is optional due to environment constraints, feature flags, or other factors. A capabilities layer would make that behavior explicit and easier to integrate into automated tooling.

**Expected Outcomes**

- A manifest file per dialect (for example, tests/<dialect>/manifest.json) recording test file and test-case tags, required capabilities, and brief notes or links for implementers.
- A linter/validator for the manifest, with CI checks in the Test Suite to keep metadata valid and in sync with tests.
- A JSON Schema describing the manifest itself, published alongside the suite.
- Migration of a meaningful subset of optional/ tests to use capability tags, starting with optional/format and a small number of other ambiguous categories.
- Documentation for runner authors explaining how to read the manifest and filter tests by capabilities.
- Optionally, a simple reference script or Bowtie example that demonstrates capability-based selection in practice.

**Likely Repositories Involved**

- json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite (manifest design, CI, and test metadata)
- bowtie-json-schema/bowtie (using the manifest for test selection and providing examples)
- json-schema-org/community (coordination and higher-level documentation)

**Skills Required**

- Familiarity with the JSON Schema Test Suite layout and contribution workflow
- JSON tooling in Python and/or Node.js for validation and CI integration
- Understanding of how format and other optional features differ across drafts and implementations

** Mentor(s)**
To be determined / seeking mentors.
​
**Expected Difficulty**
Medium.
​
**Expected Time Commitment**
Approximately 175 hours, covering the manifest design, validation tooling and CI wiring, migration of an initial set of tests, and documentation for implementers and runner authors.

issues.title: Add tests for empty and null value edge cases
issues.body:
The suite does not yet systematically cover empty arrays, empty objects, empty strings, and null instances across the relevant keywords. These are common real‑world edge cases, and adding targeted tests would help validators behave consistently for cases like optional fields and empty collections. Many core keywords may be affected (for example type, minItems / maxItems, minLength / maxLength, and required).

Proposed scope
Review existing tests for key structural and validation keywords and identify where empty / null cases are missing (empty [], {}, "", and null as instance values).

Add minimal schemas plus instances that include those values, with both valid and invalid outcomes where the schema allows either.

Follow existing test patterns and directory structure, adding new cases to all applicable draft versions, and keep each test small and focused.

If this direction seems reasonable, I’d be happy to work on a PR adding these tests.

issues.title: Add boundary value tests for numeric keywords
issues.body:
The current numeric keyword tests (`minimum`, `maximum`, `multipleOf`, `exclusiveMinimum`, `exclusiveMaximum`) have limited boundary-value coverage for very large/small numbers and precision limits. Expanding these tests would help ensure validators handle numeric edge cases correctly across different implementations and platforms.

## Current tests

Representative numeric keyword test files across drafts:

- **draft-2020-12**: [`tests/draft2020-12/minimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/minimum.json), [`tests/draft2020-12/maximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/maximum.json), [`tests/draft2020-12/multipleOf.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/multipleOf.json), [`tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMinimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMinimum.json), [`tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMaximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMaximum.json)
- **draft-2019-09**: [`tests/draft2019-09/minimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2019-09/minimum.json), [`tests/draft2019-09/maximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2019-09/maximum.json), [`tests/draft2019-09/multipleOf.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2019-09/multipleOf.json), [`tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMinimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMinimum.json), [`tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMaximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMaximum.json)
- **draft-07**: [`tests/draft7/minimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft7/minimum.json), [`tests/draft7/maximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft7/maximum.json), [`tests/draft7/multipleOf.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft7/multipleOf.json), [`tests/draft7/exclusiveMinimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft7/exclusiveMinimum.json), [`tests/draft7/exclusiveMaximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft7/exclusiveMaximum.json)
- **draft-06**: [`tests/draft6/minimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft6/minimum.json), [`tests/draft6/maximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft6/maximum.json), [`tests/draft6/multipleOf.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft6/multipleOf.json), [`tests/draft6/exclusiveMinimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft6/exclusiveMinimum.json), [`tests/draft6/exclusiveMaximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft6/exclusiveMaximum.json)
- **draft-04**: [`tests/draft4/minimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft4/minimum.json), [`tests/draft4/maximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft4/maximum.json), [`tests/draft4/multipleOf.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft4/multipleOf.json)
- **draft-03**: [`tests/draft3/minimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft3/minimum.json), [`tests/draft3/maximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft3/maximum.json)
- **v1**: [`tests/v1/minimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/v1/minimum.json), [`tests/v1/maximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/v1/maximum.json), [`tests/v1/multipleOf.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/v1/multipleOf.json), [`tests/v1/exclusiveMinimum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/v1/exclusiveMinimum.json), [`tests/v1/exclusiveMaximum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/v1/exclusiveMaximum.json)

## Proposed additions

- Large integers near JavaScript's `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` (2^53 − 1) and `Number.MIN_SAFE_INTEGER` (−2^53 + 1) for `minimum`/`maximum`/`exclusiveMinimum`/`exclusiveMaximum`
- Very small floating-point numbers near zero (e.g., 1e-308, -1e-308) for boundary validation
- Precision limit edge cases for `multipleOf` (e.g., very small divisors, floating-point precision boundaries)
- Large floating-point numbers approaching implementation limits

## Implementation notes

- Use minimal schemas focusing on the numeric keyword being tested
- Add tests to all applicable drafts (draft-03 through draft-2020-12, and v1 where the keyword exists)
- Tests for implementation-specific numeric precision limits (e.g., beyond IEEE 754 double precision) should be placed in `optional/` directories, following the pattern of [`optional/bignum.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/optional/bignum.json) and [`optional/float-overflow.json`](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/optional/float-overflow.json)

issues.title: Add edge case tests for exclusiveMinimum and exclusiveMaximum keywords
issues.body:
The `exclusiveMinimum` and `exclusiveMaximum` test files currently contain only a single basic scenario each for all draft versions. Coverage is limited to basic positive floats (e.g. `exclusiveMinimum: 1.1`, `exclusiveMaximum: 3.0`), with no tests for zero, negatives, integer boundaries, very large or very small numbers, or interaction with `minimum` / `maximum`. Expanding these tests would help validator implementations handle boundary conditions correctly and align coverage with the richer edge cases already present in `minimum.json` and `maximum.json`.

---

### Motivation

Implementations need to correctly handle exclusive bounds in a variety of numeric scenarios. The specification defines `exclusiveMinimum` and `exclusiveMaximum` for numeric validation; the suite should exercise integer boundaries, zero, negative numbers, and (where appropriate) extreme values. Tests that combine `exclusiveMinimum`/`exclusiveMaximum` with `minimum`/`maximum` would also clarify expected behaviour when both are present. Better coverage here reduces the risk of validators misbehaving on real-world schemas that use these keywords.

### Current tests

Each of the following files contains only **one test case** (one schema plus its tests):

**exclusiveMinimum**

- [tests/draft7/exclusiveMinimum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft7/exclusiveMinimum.json)
- [tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMinimum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMinimum.json)
- [tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMinimum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMinimum.json)
- [tests/v1/exclusiveMinimum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/v1/exclusiveMinimum.json)

**exclusiveMaximum**

- [tests/draft7/exclusiveMaximum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft7/exclusiveMaximum.json)
- [tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMaximum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMaximum.json)
- [tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMaximum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMaximum.json)
- [tests/v1/exclusiveMaximum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/v1/exclusiveMaximum.json)

**Affected drafts:** draft-07, draft-2019-09, draft-2020-12, and v1. Representative links: [draft7](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft7/exclusiveMinimum.json), [draft2019-09](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2019-09/exclusiveMinimum.json), [draft2020-12](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/exclusiveMinimum.json), [v1](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/v1/exclusiveMinimum.json).

### Proposed additions

- **Integer boundaries:** Test cases where the bound is an integer (e.g. `exclusiveMinimum: 0`, `exclusiveMaximum: 10`) and instances use both integer and float representations (e.g. `0` vs `0.0`, `10` vs `10.0`) to assert correct exclusive comparison.
- **Zero:** Explicit tests for zero as either the bound or the instance (e.g. `exclusiveMinimum: 0` with valid `0.1` and invalid `0`; `exclusiveMaximum: 0` with valid `-0.1` and invalid `0`).
- **Negative numbers:** Test cases with negative bounds and/or negative instances (e.g. `exclusiveMinimum: -2`, `exclusiveMaximum: -1`) to cover signed comparisons.
- **Very large and very small numbers:** Edge cases with large or small finite values (within normal JSON number range). Implementation-specific overflow behavior may follow existing optional patterns (e.g. [tests/draft2020-12/optional/bignum.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/optional/bignum.json), [tests/draft2020-12/optional/float-overflow.json](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/tests/draft2020-12/optional/float-overflow.json)) where relevant.
- **Interaction with `minimum` / `maximum`:** Test cases where both `minimum` and `exclusiveMinimum`, or both `maximum` and `exclusiveMaximum`, appear in the same schema, to assert that implementations apply the exclusive bound correctly when used together.

### Implementation notes

- Follow the [CONTRIBUTING](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) guidelines: use **minimal** schemas (only keywords under test) and prefer simpler instances where possible.
- Include both **valid** and **invalid** instances for each new test case where the schema allows either outcome; see the [README](https://github.com/json-schema-org/JSON-Schema-Test-Suite/blob/main/README.md) test structure.
- Add new test cases to **all applicable draft versions** (draft-07, draft-2019-09, draft-2020-12, v1), with any draft-specific adjustments (e.g. `$id` vs `id`, `$schema` usage) as needed.
- Before adding tests, review the spec sections for [exclusiveMinimum](https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/draft-bhutton-json-schema-validation-01#name-exclusiveminimum) and [exclusiveMaximum](https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/draft-bhutton-json-schema-validation-01#name-exclusivemaximum) to ensure the new cases match specified behavior.
